repos.name: baryrat
repos.language: Pure Python (numpy/scipy; optional gmpy2/flamp for extended precision)
repos.activity: Maintained, CI configured, ~114 commits
repos.what_it_does: Barycentric rational/polynomial approximation: AAA, plus BRASIL (best minimax rational approximation), rational interpolation with fixed poles/nodes, Floater-Hormann.
repos.modeling_approach: AAA + BRASIL minimax. Exposes polres() (pole-residue extraction) and zeros(). No state-space realization, no passivity, no circuit emit.
repos.reusable_for_us: Permissive-license alternative/complement to scipy.AAA for NEXT task 1. Its polres() gives us poles/residues to feed our RLC+controlled-source synthesizer, and BRASIL (best minimax) is attractive when we want a guaranteed-low-error LOW-ORDER fit of PSRR phase rather than least-squares — fewer sections = simpler synthesized netlist = friendlier PSS/HB. BSD-2 means we can vendor it.
repos.caveats: No passivity guarantee or enforcement, no complex-conjugate-pair structuring for signed 2nd-order sections (we structure ourselves), and no vector/shared-pole fit. Smaller/niche project than scikit-rf.

repos.name: Vector_Fitting_for_python (SebastianL18) / vectfit_python (PhilReinhold)
repos.license: RESTRICTIVE / non-OSS — SebastianL18 README forbids embedding in commercial software and lacks an OSI license; PhilReinhold's vectfit_python is an older MIT-ish port with no recent activity
repos.activity: Low — ~30 commits, no release cadence (Sebastian); PhilReinhold port effectively dormant
repos.what_it_does: Direct Python ports of Gustavsen-Semlyen Fast Relaxed Vector Fitting (vectfit3.m). Outputs both state-space (real block-diagonal for conjugate pairs) and pole-residue form.
repos.modeling_approach: Classic relaxed vector fitting; real block-diagonal state-space realization of complex-conjugate pairs is a useful pattern. No passivity enforcement, no SPICE/VA export.
repos.reusable_for_us: Reference-only. scikit-rf's vector fitting supersedes these (it adds passivity + SPICE export under a clean BSD-3 license). The block-diagonal real-state realization of conjugate pairs is a helpful illustration for our signed 2nd-order sections, but copying SebastianL18's code is blocked by its no-commercial-embedding clause.
repos.caveats: Licensing is the dealbreaker for Sebastian's (no-commercial-embedding). No passivity, no circuit emit. Prefer scikit-rf or scipy.AAA instead.

repos.name: ML circuit macromodel -> Verilog-A (Rosenbaum/UIUC/CAEML RNN; neural-ODE circuit transient lineage)
repos.license: N/A — no public code release found
repos.language: N/A (papers describe PyTorch/LSTM training + hand-written Verilog-A export)
repos.activity: Paper-only; repeated searches (CAEML, GitHub topics) found NO maintained open repo for the RNN/neural-ODE-to-Verilog-A flow
repos.what_it_does: Body of work (Chen/Raginsky/Rosenbaum EPEPS 2017 'Verilog-A compatible RNN model'; later input-to-state-stable neural-ODE transient models; high-speed-channel RNN) that trains discrete-time RNN/LSTM or neural ODEs on transient data, converts to continuous time, and emits Verilog-A black-box models for SPICE — ~5x speedups claimed.
repos.modeling_approach: Black-box NONLINEAR data-driven state-space (RNN/LSTM/neural-ODE) -> Verilog-A. Aimed at nonlinear transient behavior (I/O buffers, ESD, channels). No explicit Zout/PSRR/noise/spur decomposition; PSRR/noise/spurs are not addressed.
repos.reusable_for_us: Reference-only / largely a MISMATCH for us. Our spur band is linear (memory: harmonics <-62 dBc) so an LTI multiport (Zout+PSRR) is the right model and a nonlinear NN macromodel is overkill, harder to make PSS/HB-convergent, and risks spurious tones. Worth knowing as the citation lineage if Target B ever shows genuine nonlinearity, but there's no code to lift and the approach doesn't fit our LTI-supply, frequency-domain, passivity-gated pipeline.
repos.caveats: No public, maintained code repository despite the prominent papers — cannot be adopted. NN-to-Verilog-A models are notoriously fragile for PSS/HB convergence and don't naturally give noise/spur fidelity; orthogonal to our hard R/L/C-only constraint.

repos.name: chaospy
repos.activity: Maintained, widely used UQ toolbox
repos.what_it_does: Polynomial chaos expansion + advanced Monte Carlo for uncertainty quantification: low-discrepancy sampling, quadrature, orthogonal-polynomial surrogates.
repos.modeling_approach: Polynomial-chaos/quadrature surrogates of scalar/vector outputs vs random parameters. Not a dynamic/frequency-domain or circuit-realization tool.
repos.reusable_for_us: Reference-only / tangential for the core model, but a plausible enabler for PVT/Monte-Carlo parameterization: if we later want our OP-parameterized model coefficients (pole/residue values vs load current, temperature, process) as cheap surrogates, chaospy can build the response surface. Not relevant to Zout/PSRR/noise/spur fidelity itself.
repos.caveats: Solves a different problem (parametric UQ, not LTI fitting/realization). No circuit/Verilog-A emit. Only pull in if/when we add a PVT corner surrogate layer.

repos.name: GPflow / GPy (Gaussian-process surrogate enablers)
repos.language: Python (GPflow on TensorFlow>=2.4 + TF-Probability; GPy on numpy/scipy)
repos.activity: GPflow actively maintained (v2.11.0, Jun 2026); GPy mature/maintenance-mode
repos.what_it_does: General Gaussian-process regression frameworks (composable kernels/likelihoods, sparse/variational GPs).
repos.modeling_approach: GP regression surrogates of smooth functions. Not frequency-domain rational fitting, no circuit realization.
repos.reusable_for_us: Reference-only for the LTI model; possible enabler for smooth interpolation of model coefficients across load current / PVT (alternative to chaospy) if simple table+spline proves insufficient. Adds a heavy TensorFlow dependency (GPflow) — GPy is lighter if we ever need it.
repos.caveats: Heavyweight for what is essentially 1-D coefficient interpolation over load current; scipy splines likely suffice. No bearing on Zout/PSRR/noise/spur synthesis or the R/L/C-only constraint.

repos.name: dwarning/VA-Models (Verilog-A compact-model collection)
repos.license: Mixed (per-model; many CMC/public compact models, generally redistributable)
repos.activity: Maintained collection, used as OpenVAF/ngspice test suite
repos.what_it_does: Curated set of Verilog-A compact/behavioral models with build/run scripts validated against ngspice, Xyce, and OpenVAF.
repos.modeling_approach: Hand-written Verilog-A device/behavioral models; demonstrates idioms that compile cleanly in OpenVAF/Spectre.
repos.reusable_for_us: Reference for our Verilog-A MIRROR (.va) emit: concrete, OpenVAF/ngspice/Xyce-validated Verilog-A coding patterns and a ready test-harness structure to confirm our emitted .va compiles. Directly helps the 'must compile (OpenVAF/Spectre)' requirement and the Xyce/Qucs-S HB-validation path. Reference-only (we're not lifting model math).
repos.caveats: Device-model focused, not LDO/regulator behavioral models; no Zout/PSRR/noise/spur examples. Value is in VA idioms + cross-simulator test scaffolding, not reusable model code.

scans.takeaways: For our LTI-supply PSS/HB use case the clear winner is scikit-rf's VectorFitting (BSD-3, actively maintained): it does auto-order rational fitting with complex-conjugate pole pairs, has a built-in passivity test+enforcement (our NEXT task 2 gate), and exports a SPICE subcircuit using ONLY R/L/C + B-type controlled sources with no Laplace blocks, already targeting ngspice/Xyce — i.e. it nearly matches our hard constraint and pipeline out of the box. Round it out with scipy.interpolate.AAA / baryrat (clean licenses) and polyrat (GPL, reference-only) for auto-order and stabilized-SK fitting ideas to tighten non-min-phase PSRR/Zout phase; we still add OP-parameterization, explicit e^-sTau all-pass delay, and noise/spur blocks ourselves since none of these tools model those. The ML circuit-macromodel lineage (Rosenbaum/UIUC RNN-to-Verilog-A, neural-ODE transient) is paper-only with no maintained public code and is a conceptual mismatch — it targets nonlinear transient, whereas our spur band is linear, so it's reference-only at best. GP/PCE surrogates (GPflow Apache-2.0, GPy/chaospy BSD/MIT) and dwarning/VA-Models are useful enablers (PVT coefficient surrogates; OpenVAF/Xyce-validated Verilog-A idioms for our .va mirror) but not core to the model.

ranking.ecosystem_summary:
The open-source landscape splits cleanly into three layers, and only two of them contain anything we should actually adopt. Layer 1 = rational FITTERS (vector fitting, AAA, Sanathanan-Koerner). This is mature, permissively licensed, pure-numpy/scipy, and directly serves our hardest open boundary (Task 1 PSRR/Zout phase). Layer 2 = a PASSIVITY GATE: scikit-rf packages the canonical Gustavsen-Semlyen half-size singularity-matrix test + SVD residue-perturbation enforcement under BSD-3, which is essentially our Task-2 gate ready to call. Layer 3 = open HB/PSS ENGINES + the Verilog-A toolchain (Xyce, VACASK, OpenVAF-reloaded), which give us a Cadence-independent way to HB-validate sideband asymmetry and to compile our .va mirror, closing the gap that ngspice has no PSS/HB.

The single most important practical finding from inspecting our own repo: scipy 1.17.1 is already in the venv and ALREADY ships scipy.interpolate.AAA with .poles()/.residues(). That is a zero-dependency, zero-license-risk, auto-order rational fitter available to us today — it is the cheapest possible first move on Task 1. scikit-rf and baryrat are NOT currently installed; adopting either is a deliberate pip-install decision, not a free lunch.

A hard, repeated lesson across all six scans: every open-source SPICE EMITTER we found (scikit-rf's write_spice_subcircuit_s, SignalIntegrity, the SINTEF ATP-EMTP exporter) realizes a generic S-parameter state-space network with port reference resistors and 1F/1H normalizing elements + VCCS/CCCS — verified Laplace-free, but NOT our per-block physical realization (Zout as physical RLC branches; PSRR as signed real-pole coupling-current sections injected at vout; decoupled Norton noise; deterministic SIN spurs; OP-parameterization by load current; plus a hand-written Verilog-A mirror). None of them model PSRR-vs-Vin, noise PSD, spurs, or load-current parameterization. So the correct architecture is unambiguous: adopt OSS for FITTING and the PASSIVITY CHECK, feed the resulting poles/residues into OUR existing synthesizer (harness/fit_model.py psrr_model / zmodel / emit / emit_va), and keep our emitter and all four behavioral blocks bespoke.

Licensing materially constrains choices because we emit vendor-facing deliverables. The fitters we want are clean (scipy BSD-3 already vendored; scikit-rf BSD-3; baryrat BSD-2). The GPL-3 options (polyrat's stabilized-SK; the vectfit3 ports that are GPL or no-license/non-commercial) are usable only as local, non-distributed tools or as algorithm references to reimplement — never vendored into a shippable emitter. The HB engines (Xyce GPLv3, VACASK AGPL, OpenVAF GPL) are fine because we run them as external subprocesses exactly like ngspice; their licenses do not touch the netlists/models we generate.

top_reusable.name: scipy.interpolate.AAA (already in our venv, scipy 1.17.1)
top_reusable.why: I verified scipy.interpolate.AAA imports cleanly in our .venv (scipy 1.17.1). It is an auto-order barycentric rational fitter with .poles()/.residues(), native complex-data support, and clean_up() for Froissart doublets. ZERO new dependency, ZERO license risk, callable in fit_model.py today. This is the cheapest possible upgrade path for the auto-order Zout/PSRR fit we want.
top_reusable.use_for: Zout fit (auto-order, sharp resonances) + PSRR fit (non-min-phase poles -> feed our signed-section synthesizer); poles/residues plug straight into psrr_model G-bank and zmodel branches.
top_reusable.risk: AAA does not enforce stability/passivity/min-phase: must reflect RHP poles, drop tiny residues, and enforce conjugate symmetry before realization. No SPICE/VA emit and no delay extraction (both stay ours). Already mitigated in part by our existing pole-validation logic in _sk_fit.

top_reusable.name: scikit-rf VectorFitting (passivity_test / passivity_enforce + auto_fit)
top_reusable.why: The single best match for Task 2. Its passivity_test() is the exact Gustavsen-Semlyen half-size singularity matrix you named (source builds P=(A-B inv(D-I) C)(A-B inv(D+I) C) and checks imaginary eigenvalues); passivity_enforce() does SVD residue perturbation. BSD-3, pure numpy/scipy, drops into our 3.11 venv. auto_fit() also gives vector fitting with native complex-conjugate (2nd-order) pole pairs as a cross-check to AAA. NOT currently installed (pip install scikit-rf).
top_reusable.use_for: Passivity gate on synthesized Zout (Task 2) + alternative auto-order Zout/PSRR fitter (Task 1, cross-check AAA). Apply passivity ONLY to Zout immittance, never PSRR.
top_reusable.risk: PSRR is a non-passive transfer ratio (>1) -> NEVER run passivity_enforce on it. Passivity API is S-param-documented: must feed our 1-port Zout as Z/Y correctly (positive-real test), and verify the gate on our HAND-BUILT RLC, not just on skrf's internal realization. Do NOT reuse write_spice_subcircuit_s (S-param state-space, wrong topology for us). New dependency to install/audit.

top_reusable.name: Xyce (multi-tone Harmonic Balance)
top_reusable.why: The only mature open engine with real single- AND multi-tone .HB. Runs our exact element set (R/L/C + E/G/F/H + B-sources) natively and ingests Verilog-A. Lets us inject a ~304 MHz carrier + supply tones and read sideband/spur amplitudes WITHOUT Cadence in the loop. Our spur band is linear (memory: harmonics <-62 dBc) so HB converges well. Prebuilt Windows binaries exist; run as a subprocess like ngspice (GPL never touches our netlists).
top_reusable.use_for: HB validation of sideband asymmetry for both the SPICE subckt and the .va mirror (Task 3); cross-check Spectre PSS/HB before Cadence.
top_reusable.risk: GPLv3 (fine as external validator, do not vendor/link). HB conventions differ from Spectre -> corroborates, not bit-identical. Heavier Windows setup than ngspice; multi-tone .HB card syntax differs from Spectre. Verilog-A path needs an ADMS C++ plugin build (verify HB+VA works together) -> for the .va specifically, VACASK/OpenVAF-reloaded is the lighter route.

top_reusable.name: baryrat (AAA + BRASIL minimax)
top_reusable.why: Permissive superset of scipy.AAA: adds BRASIL best-uniform (minimax) rational approximation on top of AAA, with polres()/zeros(). Minimax matters specifically for our spur-fidelity goal because it minimizes WORST-CASE error in a band (e.g. near 304 MHz) and tends to give a LOWER-ORDER fit -> fewer sections -> simpler, friendlier PSS/HB netlist. BSD-2 means we can even vendor it.
top_reusable.use_for: PSRR/Zout fit when we want guaranteed-low-order or worst-case-bounded sections (Task 1); polres() feeds our synthesizer.
top_reusable.risk: Marginal value OVER scipy.AAA since AAA is already in-venv; justified only if minimax/low-order specifically helps. No passivity/stability enforcement (pair with skrf gate). BRASIL is primarily scalar-real-interval; for complex phase you mostly use its AAA path. New (small) dependency.

top_reusable.name: OpenVAF-reloaded + VACASK
top_reusable.why: OpenVAF-reloaded has PREBUILT WINDOWS binaries (matches our Win/3.11 stack) and compiles our ddt-only, no-Laplace, controlled-source .va to OSDI. VACASK (same author) is the only fully-open path that BOTH runs Verilog-A behavioral models AND has a working HB engine. Together: emit .va -> OpenVAF-reloaded -> .osdi -> VACASK hb gives a Cadence-independent HB check of our .va mirror, and ngspice (v39+) can load the same .osdi for AC/tran parity. Compiler/sim GPL/AGPL never touch our generated models.
top_reusable.use_for: Compile-and-check gate for the emitted .va mirror (Task 3) + open HB validation of the .va specifically; closes the 'VA is untested locally' open item from HANDOFF.
top_reusable.risk: VACASK is Codeberg-hosted, Linux-first (check Windows build / WSL), newer/less battle-tested than Spectre; no Spectre-netlist parity (port the TB). OpenVAF rejects some behavioral constructs -> keep .va in the supported ddt/controlled-source subset (we already do). HB benefit needs OSDI-0.4-consuming sim (VACASK), not ngspice.

ranking.how_others_model_ldo:
There is a sharp divide, and it validates our approach by absence. The OSS LDO-GENERATOR ecosystem (OpenFASOC ldo-gen, AnalogGym, sky130_cw_ip, ALIGN) does NOT build behavioral macromodels at all. They emit transistor-level netlists/GDS and CHARACTERIZE them via testbenches. Their characterization is also narrow relative to ours: AnalogGym's perf_extraction_LDO.py parses ngspice raw output for ~15 DC/AC/transient metrics (load/line reg, PSRR-AC sweep, DC gain, GBW, phase margin, transient under/overshoot) but explicitly does NOT extract output impedance Zout or noise. OpenFASOC's LDO is a DIGITAL LDO characterized transient-only (VREG vs clock freq vs Cout) with no continuous-time PSRR/Zout/noise concept at all. So the exact quantities our model is built on -- Zout(s), noise PSD shape, and discrete spurs -- are precisely what every OSS LDO project omits. That is our differentiator, confirmed across all six scans: nobody open-source extracts Zout/noise/spurs from an LDO and fits a behavioral supply model.

Where OSS DOES have a coherent modeling philosophy is in the macromodeling/SI world, and it matches ours conceptually. The Mike Brinson Qucs modular opamp macromodels are the closest published example: they build opamp PSRR (via auxiliary networks), output impedance, and noise from controlled sources + lumped elements, explicitly designed to survive HB -- the same per-block, controlled-source, HB-robust philosophy as our Zout/PSRR/noise/spur decomposition. The difference is they hand-design the blocks; we DATA-FIT them from a ground-truth and gate them. The rational-macromodeling community (scikit-rf, baryrat, polyrat, the DEPACT delay-extraction recipe) handles the LINEAR part the way we do -- pole/residue fit then lumped realization -- but they target generic passive multiports/interconnect, so they fit immittance/S-params and synthesize a single generic state-space network. They have no notion of a regulator's two distinct paths (driving-point Zout that should be passivity-gated vs. a PSRR transfer ratio that should NOT), no noise/spur decomposition, and no OP-parameterization by load current. The ML-macromodel lineage (UIUC/Rosenbaum RNN-to-Verilog-A, neural-ODE) is a true mismatch: it targets nonlinear transient black-boxes, has no public code, and would be fragile in PSS/HB -- exactly wrong for our verified-linear spur band.
ranking.mapping_to_our_tasks:
TASK 1 (non-min-phase PSRR/Zout phase + 2nd-order sections + delay). Today our _sk_fit (harness/fit_model.py:147) explicitly REJECTS complex poles (returns None when |pole.imag| is non-negligible -> falls back to the min-phase shelf). That is the precise gap. Plan: (a) Adopt scipy.interpolate.AAA NOW (already in-venv) to fit i_c = H/Zout for PSRR and Zout directly, keeping its complex-conjugate poles instead of discarding them. (b) Extend our psrr_model G-bank: currently it is G0 + sum Gi/(1+s/wi) (signed REAL poles only); add signed 2nd-order (complex-conjugate) sections realized as inductor-free RLC + VCCS so non-min-phase notch phase is exact. AAA/baryrat polres() supplies the conjugate pairs; OUR synthesizer realizes them. (c) Cross-check with scikit-rf auto_fit (native conjugate pairs) on the same Zout/PSRR arrays. (d) DELAY: we already have a Hilbert min-phase/linear-delay DIAGNOSTIC (bringup.py:_minphase_score, line 34 removes the best-fit linear/delay term) but no e^-sTau SYNTHESIS. No OSS implements this (DEPACT is paper-only, no code). Implement DEPACT's recipe ourselves: extract the linear-phase slope = tau, then realize e^-sTau as a lumped Bessel/Pade all-pass (RLC + controlled sources). DEPACT is the design reference, not a dependency.

TASK 2 (passivity gate on synthesized Zout). Greenfield -- no passivity code exists in our harness. Adopt scikit-rf passivity_test() (half-size Hamiltonian/singularity matrix) + is_passive() + passivity_enforce() (SVD residue perturbation). Feed it our Zout poles/residues as a 1-port immittance (positive-real test). CRITICAL: gate/enforce ONLY Zout; never the PSRR transfer ratio. Add it to score.py as a hard gate after Zout synthesis and verify it on OUR hand-built RLC values, not just on skrf's internal realization.

TASK 3 (Target B + HB validation). For IMPORT: if Cadence Zout/PSRR come out as 2-port/Touchstone, use scikit-rf Network Z/Y conversion + IEEEP370 de-embedding to clean to Z(f) before fitting; for our DC-extracted ground truth this is optional. The extraction/fit/emit pipeline itself (import_cadence.py CSV->gt_ref.npz, then existing fit+emit) stays ours -- no OSS does Zout/PSRR/noise/spur extraction. For HB VALIDATION of sideband asymmetry: run the emitted subckt under Xyce multi-tone .HB (304 MHz carrier + supply tones). For the .va mirror specifically (closing the 'VA untested locally' open item): OpenVAF-reloaded (Windows binary) -> .osdi -> ngspice v39+ for AC/tran parity, and -> VACASK hb for an open HB check. Qucs-S is an optional GUI to prewire the Xyce .HB cards.
ranking.gaps_no_oss: Five pieces have NO usable OSS and stay bespoke. (1) Zout/PSRR/noise/spur EXTRACTION from an LDO: every OSS LDO project (AnalogGym, OpenFASOC, sky130_cw_ip) omits Zout and noise extraction entirely; our bench.py + gen_reference.py is the differentiator. (2) Our per-block PHYSICAL realization + emitter: all OSS emitters (scikit-rf write_spice_subcircuit_s, SignalIntegrity, SINTEF) produce a generic S-parameter state-space network with port reference resistors -- not our physical Zout RLC branches, signed-section PSRR coupling current at vout, decoupled Norton noise, or deterministic SIN spurs. Keep harness/fit_model.py emit/psrr_model/zmodel. (3) The explicit e^-sTau ALL-PASS delay extraction+synthesis: DEPACT describes the recipe but has no maintained open code; we implement the lumped Bessel/Pade all-pass ourselves. (4) NOISE Norton block (white + Lorentzians, In=Sv/|Zout|) and the SPUR block (deterministic vout tones): no OSS models LDO output-noise PSD shape or discrete spurs -- both are entirely ours. (5) OP-PARAMETERIZATION by load current (poles/residues interpolated quad-in-ln(iload)): no rational-fit library has any notion of a parametric sweep coupled to circuit realization (polyrat has multivariate SK and chaospy/GP could surface-fit coefficients later, but that is overkill vs. our existing spline/quadratic interpolation). The Verilog-A MIRROR content is also ours (dwarning/VA-Models gives only device-model idioms and a build harness, no regulator behavioral patterns).
ranking.recommendation:
Adopt three things, in this order, and keep everything else in-house.

1. IMMEDIATELY (zero cost, zero license risk): use scipy.interpolate.AAA -- already importable in our venv (scipy 1.17.1) -- as the auto-order Zout/PSRR fitter for Task 1. Keep its complex-conjugate poles (our _sk_fit currently discards them, which is the documented Task-1 gap) and feed them to an EXTENDED psrr_model that gains signed 2nd-order (complex-conjugate) RLC+VCCS sections. This is the highest-leverage, lowest-risk move.

2. INSTALL scikit-rf (BSD-3) for two jobs: (a) Task-2 passivity gate -- passivity_test (half-size Hamiltonian) + passivity_enforce, applied ONLY to synthesized Zout, never PSRR; (b) auto_fit as a vector-fitting cross-check against AAA. Do NOT use its SPICE exporter -- wrong topology for us.

3. INSTALL Xyce (GPLv3, run as external subprocess) for Task-3 HB validation of sideband asymmetry near 304 MHz, plus OpenVAF-reloaded (Windows binary) + VACASK to compile and HB-check the .va mirror (closes the 'VA untested locally' open item) -- since ngspice has no PSS/HB.

Treat as REFERENCE-ONLY (do not vendor): baryrat (only if minimax/low-order specifically helps -- AAA already covers the base case), polyrat's stabilized-SK (GPL-3; reimplement the published algorithm if our SK conditioning needs it), the vectfit3 ports (GPL/no-license), DEPACT (paper recipe for our own all-pass), AnalogGym/sky130_cw_ip (extra ground-truth DUTs), and Brinson's Qucs macromodels (topology inspiration). Explicitly do not pursue: the ML/RNN-to-Verilog-A lineage (no code, nonlinear, PSS/HB-fragile, mismatched to our verified-linear spur band) and any OSS SPICE emitter. Net: OSS gives us a better fitter and a real passivity gate cheaply, plus an open HB cross-check; the extraction, the four behavioral blocks, the physical synthesizer, the delay all-pass, and the OP-parameterization remain our defensible core.
